title: Distributed Quantum Error Correction with Bivariate Bicycle Codes in a Modular Architecture
authors: Nitish Kumar Chandra, Eneet Kaur, Reza Nejabati, Kaushik P. Seshadreesan
abstract: Quantum low density parity check (qLDPC) codes, particularly bivariate bicycle (BB) codes, achieve competitive fault tolerance thresholds while offering substantially higher encoding rates than planar surface codes. However, their intrinsically long-range stabilizer structure makes them difficult to implement on monolithic devices with nearest neighbor connectivity and limited qubit capacity. In this work, we study the realization of a BB code in a modular multiprocessor architecture, where quantum processors are interconnected through shared Bell pairs. We consider processors with all to all internal connectivity, which is feasible on trapped ion and neutral atom platforms, enabling flexible local gate execution while inter-processor (nonlocal) gates are mediated by shared entanglement. We describe a star network architecture that can realize this distributed setting. We partition the qubits of the [[144,12,12]] BB code across 4, 6, and 12 quantum processors and analyze the resulting logical error rates and pseudo-threshold performance under circuit level noise by varying the number of processors and a scaling factor that captures the additional noise associated with nonlocal operations. We use Monte Carlo simulations with BP+OSD decoding and extend the previously known BB code ansatz to the distributed setting. Our results provide architectural insight and design considerations for distributed BB codes in modular quantum computing architectures.
summary: This paper studies the performance of high-rate Bivariate Bicycle codes when implemented in a distributed, modular quantum computing architecture. It quantifies the trade-off between splitting the code across multiple processors and the performance penalty caused by noisy nonlocal gates. The findings provide design guidelines for scaling quantum error correction in modular hardware like trapped ions or neutral atoms.
main_problem: Investigating how partitioning Bivariate Bicycle (BB) codes across a modular, distributed architecture affects their error-correction performance, specifically considering the increased noise from nonlocal operations.
main_result: The pseudo-threshold decreases as nonlocal noise increases, and coarser partitions (fewer QPUs) are more effective at higher noise levels (alpha >= 3).
method: Monte Carlo simulations using a circuit-level noise model and BP+OSD decoding, combined with a new quadratic alpha-dependent extension of the BB code ansatz for error rate extrapolation.
model_or_system: A modular star network architecture where multiple quantum processors (QPUs) are connected via a central switch using entanglement-assisted Bell pairs, applicable to trapped ion and neutral atom platforms.
important_parameters: Number of QPUs (4, 6, 12), nonlocal noise scaling factor (alpha), and the [[144, 12, 12]] BB code parameters.
main_assumptions: Processors have all-to-all internal connectivity; nonlocal noise is captured by a single scaling factor alpha; and the expansion of code coefficients is truncated at the quadratic term.
figures_summary: Circuit diagrams for syndrome extraction, schematics of remote CNOT gates and star network architecture, visualizations of code partitioning, and plots showing logical error rates and pseudo-threshold degradation.
paper_structure: The paper defines the BB code and distributed architecture, describes the noise model and circuit implementation, presents Monte Carlo simulation results and the analytical fitting ansatz, and concludes with architectural design considerations and future work.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Depth-8 syndrome measurement cycle circuit.
Fig. 2. Remote CNOT between QPU A and QPU B using a shared Bell pair. Here, 𝑐is the control qubit, 𝑡is the target qubit, and 𝑎,𝑏are communication qubits that share the Bell pair.

Fig. 3. QPU partitioning of the [[144, 12, 12]] bivariate bicycle code for 𝑁QPU = 6. The 12 𝑥-columns are divided into six blocks of width 𝑐= 2, and the highlighted cell (5, 2) lies in QPU 2. The markers show the qubit types 𝐿, 𝑋, 𝑍, and 𝑅. The overlaid blue and red edges show the 𝑋- and 𝑍-check connectivity of the highlighted cell, respectively, as defined in Eqs. (7) and (11). Edges within QPU 2 are local, while edges crossing into other QPU blocks are nonlocal. The horizontal and vertical axes label the torus cell coordinates (𝑥, 𝑦) ∈Z12 × Z6; each cell contains the four qubits 𝐿, 𝑋, 𝑍, and 𝑅.

Fig. 4. Star network architecture in which multiple QPUs (6) are connected through a central quantum switch. Each QPU contains data and ancilla qubits with intra-module connectivity, together with communication qubits used to generate entangled pairs with the switch. The switch uses these entangled pairs, together with Bell-state measurements (BSM), to enable nonlocal operations between QPUs.

Fig. 5. BB144 threshold fitting with quadratic alpha dependence. Logical error rate per cycle (𝑝𝐿) vs. physical error rate (𝑝) for different 𝛼values. The curves represent the BB ansatz fit, and asterisks mark the pseudo-thresholds 𝑝0(𝛼). The dashed lines show extrapolation to lower 𝑝.
